3: With a continued rise in the global population, the increasing de- mand for poultry and egg has resulted in a growing poultry manure production. In Singapore, 0.2 million tonnes of chicken and 2.2 billion pieces of eggs have been consumed in 2020 (Singapore food agency, 2020), which resulted in 0.35 million tonnes of chicken manure (CM). By now, the CM management strategies can be clustered into two different categories: disposal technique and energy generation tech- nique. The most common disposal technique is the direct land applica- tion, in which the raw or composted poultry waste is employed as organic soil fertilizer (Tañczuk et al., 2019). Although the rich amount of Calcium, Nitrogen, and Phosphorous in raw poultry wastes are beneficial for the soil, overuse and improper applications can result in environmental and health hazards, including the presence of pathogens (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 2010), heavy metals (Li and Shuman., 1997), and potential water irrigation (Pachepsky et al., 2011).

A national survey by the U.S. Department of Agriculture shows that 11.7% of growers utilized manure-based fertilizer in 2015/16, with 4.9% utilizing raw manure, 8.9% utilizing composted manure, and 3.4% composting manure on local farms (Astill et al., 2019). To limit micro- bial contamination, the composted manure needs to be made following specific temperature and time requirements, and the raw manure is suggested to be avoided during the last 90 days prior to harvest (Astill et al., 2019). Noted that the CM application as organic fertilizer may also contribute to the greenhouse gas (GHG) emission due to the methane emission and N2O formation (Bouwman et al., 2002). Considering the impacts of agricultural applications on both micro- bial risk and global warming potential (GWP), worldwide efforts have been focused on the energy generation approaches which employ the CM as a renewable energy source. One of the potential energy genera- tion technologies is anaerobic digestion (AD), which converts the organic matter of CM into biogas (mainly CH4 and CO2) for downstream heat and power generation. However, due to the ammonia inhibition
Fig. 1. Industrial chicken manure gasification system: (A) chicken manure gasifier; (B) pretreatment rotary dryer; (C) gas holder; (D) syngas cooling tower; (E) schematic flow diagram of the system.

effect (Pan et al., 2019) and techno-economic viability of ammonium removal techniques (Esteves et al., 2019; Jurgutis et al., 2020), most of the chicken manure AD processes cannot reach their full potential in terms of microbiological conversion. Werner Fuchs et al. reviewed the current full-scale chicken manure AD applications in Europe and China and highlighted the impact of ammonia inhibition (Fuchs et al., 2018). Apart from the AD technique, gasification (or the process of pyrolysis and gasification) is a promising solution for CM management, which converts part of the carbonaceous content into syngas as fuel and gen- erates biochar as a by-product. Although there were studies focused on pig manure gasification or dairy manure gasification, studies on poultry manure gasification are still incipient (Bora et al., 2020). Burra et al. analyzed the syngas evolutionary behavior of CM gasification and showed the difference between CM and other types of biomass, including high ash content and low heating value (Burra et al., 2016). The synergistic effects of CM co-gasification with other feedstocks such as wood (Ng et al., 2017) and petroleum coke (Liu et al., 2021) have been analyzed. However, most of the CM gasification applications are

limited to small-scale or pilot levels. Therefore, the large-scale produc- tion stability and environmental performance remain uncertain. A small modular poultry litter gasification is demonstrated in the U.S. SBIR program with a 22.5 kg/h litter feeding rate (Reardon et al., 2002). Taupe et al. reported a farm-scale CM gasification unit with a 40 kg/h feedstock feeding rate (Taupe et al., 2016). Jeswani et al. analyzed the environmental performance of a small-scale poultry manure gasification system with combined heat and power (CHP) generations (Jeswani et al., 2019). However, the impact of biochar on GHG mitigation was not considered in their environmental assessment.

Despite the potential as a sustainable waste stream, poultry manure is not fully utilized due to the lack of public confidence in both food safety and environmental concerns. To overcome the barriers of poultry manure utilization and standardize the management practice, the following research gaps need to be filled: lack of large-scale poultry manure gasification demonstration; microbial risk assessment on human health, and the environmental assessment on GWP for poultry manure gasification process.

In response to the research gaps and increase sustainability of poultry manure management, the aim of this paper is to demonstrate an industrial-level case study of CM gasification in Singapore. To analyze the energy and environmental performance of the CM gasification plant, technical data from the industrial plant is adapted into an integrated chemical reaction kinetics model to ensure the simulation outputs are realistic. The life cycle analysis is carried out to analyze the GWP per- formances of CM gasification and 2 benchmark CM management solu- tions: the AD technique and direct land application. Besides, a quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) on CM management is carried out to analyze the impact of potential pathogenic hazards. This study should be helpful to provide a circular solution for CM manage- ment to achieve a carbon-neutral future.

However, due to the ammonia inhibition
Fig. 1. Industrial chicken manure gasification system: (A) chicken manure gasifier; (B) pretreatment rotary dryer; (C) gas holder; (D) syngas cooling tower; (E) schematic flow diagram of the system.

effect (Pan et al., 2019) and techno-economic viability of ammonium removal techniques (Esteves et al., 2019; Jurgutis et al., 2020), most of the chicken manure AD processes cannot reach their full potential in terms of microbiological conversion. Werner Fuchs et al. reviewed the current full-scale chicken manure AD applications in Europe and China and highlighted the impact of ammonia inhibition (Fuchs et al., 2018). Apart from the AD technique, gasification (or the process of pyrolysis and gasification) is a promising solution for CM management, which converts part of the carbonaceous content into syngas as fuel and gen- erates biochar as a by-product. Although there were studies focused on pig manure gasification or dairy manure gasification, studies on poultry manure gasification are still incipient (Bora et al., 2020). Burra et al. analyzed the syngas evolutionary behavior of CM gasification and showed the difference between CM and other types of biomass, including high ash content and low heating value (Burra et al., 2016). The synergistic effects of CM co-gasification with other feedstocks such as wood (Ng et al., 2017) and petroleum coke (Liu et al., 2021) have been analyzed. However, most of the CM gasification applications are

limited to small-scale or pilot levels. Therefore, the large-scale produc- tion stability and environmental performance remain uncertain. A small modular poultry litter gasification is demonstrated in the U.S. SBIR program with a 22.5 kg/h litter feeding rate (Reardon et al., 2002). Taupe et al. reported a farm-scale CM gasification unit with a 40 kg/h feedstock feeding rate (Taupe et al., 2016). Jeswani et al. analyzed the environmental performance of a small-scale poultry manure gasification system with combined heat and power (CHP) generations (Jeswani et al., 2019). However, the impact of biochar on GHG mitigation was not considered in their environmental assessment.

Despite the potential as a sustainable waste stream, poultry manure is not fully utilized due to the lack of public confidence in both food safety and environmental concerns. To overcome the barriers of poultry manure utilization and standardize the management practice, the following research gaps need to be filled: lack of large-scale poultry manure gasification demonstration; microbial risk assessment on human health, and the environmental assessment on GWP for poultry manure gasification process.

In response to the research gaps and increase sustainability of poultry manure management, the aim of this paper is to demonstrate an industrial-level case study of CM gasification in Singapore. To analyze the energy and environmental performance of the CM gasification plant, technical data from the industrial plant is adapted into an integrated chemical reaction kinetics model to ensure the simulation outputs are realistic. The life cycle analysis is carried out to analyze the GWP per- formances of CM gasification and 2 benchmark CM management solu- tions: the AD technique and direct land application. Besides, a quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) on CM management is carried out to analyze the impact of potential pathogenic hazards. This study should be helpful to provide a circular solution for CM manage- ment to achieve a carbon-neutral future.
9: The industrial chicken manure gasification case study is based on the Acropower chicken manure gasification plant in Singapore, which in- cludes the pretreatment of raw CM, gasification, syngas cooling and storage, and internal combustion engine for power generation (as shown in Fig. 1). The capacity of the system is the treatment of 193 tonnes of raw CM/day, which is collected from a chicken farm producing 1.2 million eggs/day. The collected raw CM in the receiving bunker is first pretreated in a rotary dryer (as shown in Fig. 1B) to reduce the moisture content from ~90% to ~10%. The heat source of the dryer is the hot air generated by a burner employing horticultural waste (majorly wood) as feedstock. The dry CM is then pelletized into manure pellets and stored in the gasification feedstock tanks. The diameter and length of the manure pellets are 8-9 mm and 12-16 cm, respectively. The CM gasifier is an open-core downdraft fixed bed gasifier with a maximum capacity of 750 kg/h (as shown in Fig. 1A). Both the manure pellets and the air (as gasifying agent) are entering at the top of the gasifier and the air flow- rate is controlled by the pressure difference between the backpressure at the cooled syngas outlet and the atmospheric pressure at the gasifier air inlet. The solid residues (i.e., biochar) are collected at the bottom of the gasifier and the syngas is pumped out through the gasifier gas interlayer to a cyclone unit to remove solid particles in syngas. The syngas from the cyclone unit is linked to the syngas cooling tower subsystem, which includes 2 stages of air-cooling units, 2 stages of water-cooling units, and

1 optional stage of refrigeration unit (as shown in Fig. 1D). The outlet temperature of the syngas cooling tower subsystem is ~35 °C. The cooled syngas is stored in a gas holder unit (as shown in Fig. 1C), which is linked to internal combustion engines (ICE) for power generation. A schematic flow diagram of the CM gasification plant is shown in Fig. 1E.

The properties of the dry CM, a poultry litter feedstock from the UK (Jeswani et al., 2019; Palma and Martin, 2013), and the gasification generated biochar are listed in Tables A2. The dry CM has a moisture content is 9.02 wt%, which is 64% less than the UK sample. Note that low moisture content feedstocks can be helpful to maintain a high gasification temperature and a subsequent tar formation control (Yao et al., 2018). The carbon content in dry CM is 30.90% and the ash content is 25.86%, while they are 30.59% and 64.39% respectively in CM biochar. After gasification, the volatile content is reduced from 45.24% to 6.24%. Note that the Ca content is quite high (20.51%), followed by K (4.12%) and Si (1.85%) in CM biochar. Heavy metals, such as Zn, Mn, Cu, and Mo, are very limited in CM biochar. These re- sults are agreed by the former studies (Hussein et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2021). Furthermore, the pore structure analysis of the CM biochar in- dicates that the BET surface area of CM biochar is 11.25 with a total pore volume of 0.04 cm² and average pore size of 14.22 nm. The CM biochar turns out to be an environment-friendly resource due to the low heavy metal contents, and the N (2.41%) and K (4.12%) contents are considerable, which show the potential to be used as fertilizer. However, the pore structure is not comparable to be used as activated carbon (Deng et al., 2020).

14: The energy performance is evaluated based on a numerical chemical reaction kinetics model adapted to the industrial chicken manure gasi- fication plant. The gasifier model is based on a four stages framework (Ong et al., 2015; He et al., 2020), representing the main steps of the gasification process: drying, pyrolysis, oxidation, and reduction. In the drying step, the remaining moisture in the feedstock is further released after absorbing heat. The heat absorption is classified into two sub- modules. If the temperature is below the water boiling point, the drying process will be driven by the moisture concentration difference between the solid surface and the surrounding gasifying agent. When the solid temperature reaches the water boiling point, all heat flux is consumed by the isothermal water vaporization. In the pyrolysis step, a two-step biomass pyrolysis kinetic model is employed. In the first step, the dry feedstock is pyrolyzed to primary char and pyrolysis gases. In the second step, the pyrolysis gases are further reacted with char components to generate secondary char and gases. In the oxidation step, the outputs of the pyrolysis module are combusted in conditions of lack of oxygen with respect to the stoichiometric ratio. In the reduction step, the gas mixture and the char components are reacted with each other and result in the formation of gasification syngas outputs. The ash content is assumed to be inactive in all four steps (Maneerung et al., 2018). The detailed nu- merical formulation of the four-stage gasification model as well as the drying pretreatment and ICE are reported in Table A1 (Appendix). The energy performance is indicated by the cold gas efficiency (CGE), which is defined as the ratio of the produced syngas energy to the energy consumption of chicken manure feedstock and calculated based on the numerical four-stage model adapted to the industrial CM gasification plant. The CM and biochar characteristics as well as the gasification operating parameter values are reported in Tables A2 (Appendix).
15: The environmental performance is evaluated based on the indicators of ecosystem quality, climate change, and resource depletion. The per- formance of the CM gasification approach is compared to the direct land application and AD approach. The system boundaries of the 3 different CM management solutions are illustrated in Fig. 2. In the direct land application scenario, the collected raw CM is applied as soil fertilizer, avoiding the utilization of synthetic fertilizer. In the gasification sce- nario, the raw CM is first dried and pelletized into manure pellets and sent for gasification. The generated syngas is employed for power gen- eration through ICE units, supplying the plant electricity demand and avoiding fossil-fuel electricity generation in Singapore. The biochar is applied as soil amendments and thus reducing fertilizer utilization. In the AD scenario, the collected raw CM is employed by an anaerobic digester to generate biogas (majorly methane) and digestate. The biogas is utilized as the fuel of an ICE-based combine heat and power genera- tion (CHP) unit. The recovered heat from the exhaust gas and engine jacket supplies the heat demand of digestate dewatering and the elec- tricity avoids fossil fuel power generation in Singapore.

Due to the gasification plant being collocated to the chicken farm, transportation emission for raw CM collection is assumed to be negli- gible. The emissions from the manufacture of process units and trans- portation tools are not considered in this study. The raw manure methane emission rate is estimated to be 3.32 kg/tonne volatile solids (Wu et al., 2013). The N2O emissions of an average 12.5 kg N2O-N/tonne N-added after the fertilizer application are also consid- ered in this study (Bouwman et al., 2002). An average of 10 km trans- portation distance of soil application is assumed with an emission factor of 0.128 kg CO2-eq/tkm (following the Euro IV emission standard for land transportation). The emission from wood combustion was consid- ered biogenic and therefore not included in the calculation.

The substituted chemical fertilizers that are commonly used in Singapore are urea, phosphorus pentoxide, and potassium oxide. The emission factors were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Moreover, an avoided transportation distance of 760 km was considered since most of these fertilizers are otherwise imported from Malaysia. The product yields of gasification are calculated based on the validated numerical model and the biogas yield of AD is estimated to be 0.3 m³ /kg volatile solid (Chen et al., 2021). The parasitic power consumption for system operation is provided primarily by the electricity generated from ICE and the rest amount is considered as avoided general electricity from the Singapore power grid (0.4085 kg CO2-eq/kWh) (Singapore energy market authority, 2020). Emissions of each GHG were converted into carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-eq), which were calculated by multi- plying their respective GWP emission factors by the specific mass of each GHG. The emission factors of CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, based on a 100-year horizon (Pachauri et al., 2014). More detailed life-cycle inventory data of environmental performance evaluations are reported in Table A3 (Appendix).

18: To quantify the microbial impact of CM, the quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) method is employed to analyze the potential effect of microbial exposure. The QMRA in this study follows a four-step procedure: hazard pathogen identification, exposure assessment, dose- response analysis, and risk characterization.

To identify the potential hazard pathogen in the CM management process, a pathogenic bacteria analysis is performed in this study. The solid samples including raw CM, dry CM, and CM biochar (solid residues of gasification), were firstly mechanically grinded to obtain the particle size of less than 1 mm, then the powder samples were suspended in a lysis buffer by using a commercial Fast Soil DNA Kit. The DNA extraction was performed as per manufacturer protocol. The quality of the extracted DNA was assessed by using a Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Scientific). The bacterial amplicon libraries were prepared by ampli- fying the 16 S V3-V4 amplicon using 5' CCTAYGGGRBGCASCAG 3' and 5' GGACTACNNGGGTATCTAAT 3' to isolate 470bp Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) fragment. The fragment quality was tested by an Agilent 5400 Fragment Analyzer. The PCR fragment which passed the quality test was purified using a commercial kit and then arranged for the li- brary construction. For the library construction, firstly, the sequencing libraries were generated by using NEBNext Ultra DNA Library Pre Kit for Illumina, following the manufacturer's recommendations. The li- brary quality was assessed via a Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Sci- entific) and Agilent Bioanalyzer. Finally, the library was sequenced on an Illumina Novaseq platform and 150 bp paired-end reads were generated. The 16SV3-V4 amplicon was analyzed using Silva 138 release via QIIME 2.0. The Alpha diversity (Chao 1 and Shannon index) was calculated with QIIME 2.0 to explain the composition of bacterial communities. Measurement for each sample was performed two times. The exposure pathway considered in this study is the consumption of leafy green vegetable vegetables grown close to the chicken manure downstream application site. The loading rate of pathogens to leafy green vegetables grown is determined based on distance from the

Fig. 3. Syngas production from CM gasification plant: (A) experiment data of continuous plant operation; (B) comparison between the experimental and simulation data; (C) effect of equivalence ratio on syngas products.

manure application site, the surface area of the leafy green vegetable vegetables, and the modeled pathogen deposition flux. The one-time ingested pathogen dose is estimated based on the maximum pathogen loading to crop, adult daily leafy green consumption rate, and a removal barrier by washing. Refer to Tables A4 and A5 (Appendix) for more details of the pathogen exposure model.

20: To quantify the microbial impact of CM, the quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) method is employed to analyze the potential effect of microbial exposure. The QMRA in this study follows a four-step procedure: hazard pathogen identification, exposure assessment, dose- response analysis, and risk characterization.

To identify the potential hazard pathogen in the CM management process, a pathogenic bacteria analysis is performed in this study. The solid samples including raw CM, dry CM, and CM biochar (solid residues of gasification), were firstly mechanically grinded to obtain the particle size of less than 1 mm, then the powder samples were suspended in a lysis buffer by using a commercial Fast Soil DNA Kit. The DNA extraction was performed as per manufacturer protocol. The quality of the extracted DNA was assessed by using a Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Scientific). The bacterial amplicon libraries were prepared by ampli- fying the 16 S V3-V4 amplicon using 5' CCTAYGGGRBGCASCAG 3' and 5' GGACTACNNGGGTATCTAAT 3' to isolate 470bp Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) fragment. The fragment quality was tested by an Agilent 5400 Fragment Analyzer. The PCR fragment which passed the quality test was purified using a commercial kit and then arranged for the li- brary construction. For the library construction, firstly, the sequencing libraries were generated by using NEBNext Ultra DNA Library Pre Kit for Illumina, following the manufacturer's recommendations. The li- brary quality was assessed via a Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Sci- entific) and Agilent Bioanalyzer. Finally, the library was sequenced on an Illumina Novaseq platform and 150 bp paired-end reads were generated. The 16SV3-V4 amplicon was analyzed using Silva 138 release via QIIME 2.0. The Alpha diversity (Chao 1 and Shannon index) was calculated with QIIME 2.0 to explain the composition of bacterial communities. Measurement for each sample was performed two times. 22: 4.1. Energy performance of chicken manure gasification

The energy produced from the CM gasification plant mainly results from syngas. Fig. 3A shows a typical continuous operation of the gasi- fication plant for 4.5 h. The majority of the syngas components are CO,

CO2, and H2 with concentrations of 14.9%, 15.6%, and 13.75%, respectively. The majority of the rest component is N2 from the air gasification process. There is ~1.8% O2 found in the syngas output during the first 60 min and then gradually reduced to 0.3%. The decrease of O2 content leads to an increase in H2 content and a decrease in CO2 content. This is because less carbon content reacted with O2 and the reduction reactions dominate. The CnHm represents the sum of C2H2, C2H4, and other CHm with n > 3, and the mean concentration value is 2.0% (majorly C2H4 with a mean value of 1.9%).

Furthermore, the energy performance is evaluated based on the four- stage numerical model adapted to the industrial CM gasification plant. The simulation results are then compared with the mean values of transient operation profiles, as shown in Fig. 3B. The simulation outputs are consistent with the plant operation data while the numerical model

Fig. 4. Difference of environmental performance of AD and gasification compared to the direct land application (fixed at 100% for all three indicators). In the resource depletion category, a higher proportion corresponds to better performance. In the ecosystem quality and climate change category, a lower proportion corresponds to better performance.

overpredicts the CO, CO2, and H2 content by 0.90%, 1.15%, and 0.86%, respectively. There are no O2 and CnHm contents in the simulation output due to the kinetic model assumptions. The CH4 content is underpredicted by 0.17%. As the input parameter of equivalence ratio (ER) is calculated based on the N2 content in the syngas output, there is no mismatch in N2 content results. The LHV of syngas is predicted to be 4.28 MJ/m³, which is 7.8% lower than the experimental operation result (4.64 MJ/m3. This is majorly due to the 2% CnHm concentration dif- ference between the experimental operation result and numerical simulation prediction. The CGE of CM gasification is found to be 78.4%, which is consistent with the results in previous studies (Ong et al., 2015; Ng et al., 2017). Noted that in the CGE calculation, the syngas LHV is
multiplied by a constant tuning coefficient of 1.084 (which represents the ratio of the experimental syngas LHV to numerical simulation pre- diction) to offset the prediction mismatch of CHm.

ER (equivalence ratio), which combined the effects of both CM feeding rates and inlet air flowrate, is a key factor in the gasification process (Yao et al., 2020; He et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 3C, when the ER is increased from 0.1 to 0.6, the lower heat value (LHV, also known as net calorific value) is decreased from 7.6 MJ/m³ 3 to 3.6 MJ/m 3 This is due to the inert N2 content does not contribute to the heating value and the decreases of H2and CH4 contents in produced syngas. When the ER is changed from 0.1 to 0.6, a 47% reduction is found in both H2 content (from 24.9% to 11.8%) and CO2 content (from 22.4% to 10.4%). The CO
Fig. 5. Life cycle GHG emission of 3 chicken manure management solutions: (A) Base case; (B) coal power substitution effects; (C) CGE effects on CM gasification; (D) biogas yield effects on CM AD.

content decreases from 16.1% to 15.3% (when ER is increased from 0.1 to 0.3) and then increases to 15.8% (when ER is changed to 0.6). Given the inert N2 content is increased from 30.1% to 61.4%, a demotion of the water-gas shift reaction can be revealed when the ER is increased. This finding shows good consistency with studies on a downdraft fixed gasifier in literature (Mahishi and Goswami, 2007). The syngas pro- duction rate increases from 0.79 to kg/kg CM when ER is increased from 0.1 to 0.6, as shown in Fig. 3C. As the CGE is defined as the ratio of the produced syngas energy to the energy consumption of chicken manure feedstock, both the syngas generation rate and syngas LHV contribute to the energy performance indicator. The simulation results show the tradeoff between syngas production rates and syngas heating values. The CGE is first increased from 60.9% to 79.2% when the ER is increased from 0.1 to 0.45, and then decreased to 76.4% when ER rea- ches 0.6. The optimal ER is found to be 0.45 with a 79.2% CGE, which is 1.04% higher when compared to the validation point (ER : 0.37). With the optimal ER (0.45), the overall thermal efficiency of the gasification plant is found to be 73%. A similar tradeoff in CGE can be found in previous work done by Yao et al. (2018), who reported an increase in CGE when ER is increased from 0.1 to 0.25 (with an optimal CGE of 72.8% at ER = 0.25) and then decrease when ER changes to 0.5. The difference in the optimal ER value is potentially due to the types of biomass feedstock and gasifier geometries. In Yao et al.'s study, the biomass feedstock is wood chips is gasified in a pilot-scale fixed-bed downdraft gasifier with a 0.5 m height and a 0.15 m diameter (Yao et al., 2018).

28: <header>4.2. Environmental performance of the chicken manure gasification process </header>

The environmental performance is evaluated based on the indicators of ecosystem quality, climate change, and resource depletion. As shown in Fig. 4, gasification shows significant advantages in terms of climate change and resource depletion. It is important to note that better per- formance of resource depletion is portrayed through higher bars while the ecosystem quality and climate change indicators are through lower bars. The results of mid-point indicators and the characterization factors used for mid-point indicators are given in Figure A1 and Table A6 (Appendix), respectively.

The ecosystem quality results of the direct land application, AD, and gasification approaches are 204, 28.7, and -68.8 PDF m² year, respec- tively. As shown in Fig. 4, the direct land application approach has the worst performance when compared to the AD and gasification ap- proaches. This is mainly due to the nutrient flow. In the AD and gasifi- cation approaches, the avoided fertilizer provides high benefits. However, nutrient leaching and subsequent pollution have a significant negative impact on the performance of the direct land application. For the resource depletion indicator, the gasification approach performs favorably by a 260% improvement when compared to the direct land application (5948 and 1659 MJ, respectively). The amount of heat and power generated from gasification is more than its life cycle energy consumption.

The climate change results of the CM gasification approach, direct land application, and AD approach are -82.7, 76.2, and 38.3 kg CO2-eq/ tonne CM, respectively (as shown in Fig. 5A). In the land application scenario, although the nutrients (N, P, K) of CM can avoid utilization of synthetic fertilizer, the methane and N2O emission from raw CM has a higher impact on the indicator of the life cycle GHG emission. The GHG emission of avoided synthetic fertilizer shipping compromise a small portion of the net impact (-5.74 kg CO2-eq/tonne CM, which is inte- grated into the avoided fertilizer bar) but is 4.5 times higher than shipping raw CM to land application sites (1.28 kg CO2-eq/tonne CM, which is integrated into the manure emission bar). The mass of the avoided synthetic fertilizer is 5.8% of the CM mass but the transport distance is much longer (760 km, assumed import from Malaysia). In the gasification scenario, the negative GHG emission result of the

gasification approach is due to credits from the biochar carbon seques- tration, avoided fertilizer, and avoided electricity generation, which contributes -218.1, -85.1, and -97.4 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The positive part is the combustion emission in the exhaust gas of ICE. Similar to the gasification approach, the AD solution also has the credits of avoided fertilizer, avoided electricity, and carbon sequestration.

Among all the integrated GHG emission categories, combustion emission plays the most important role in both gasification and AD ap- proaches (44.2% and 32.6%, respectively), and the second-largest impact is found to be the carbon sequestration categories of biochar and dewatered digestate (30.3% and 18.7%, respectively). Although the net energy of generated syngas is 8.4% higher than the biogas, the electricity generation of gasification is only 2.1% higher than the AD approach. This is because the biogas (majorly methane) has a higher heating value and a subsequent better ICE efficiency when compared to syngas. The gasification approach shows a 42.7% improvement in terms of carbon sequestration when compared to the AD approach. This is due to biochar having a higher ratio of soil sequestration than dewatered digestate. Besides, the N2O emission from digestate (161.9 kg CO2-eq/ tonne CM) also offsets the overall environmental performance of the AD approach. Based on the life cycle GHG emission results of the 3 sce- narios, the gasification approach can play an important role in GHG emission reduction.

There are several parameters that could influence the environmental performance results and the impacts of the uncertainties are discussed in this subsection. The first uncertainty considered is the potential types of substitute energy. The environmental performance results shown in Fig. 5A are assumed to replace Singapore's grid power with an average operating margin emission factor (0.4085 CO2-eq/kWh). To further understand the effects of electricity substitution, the electricity derived from coal-fired power (1.0024 CO2-eq/kWh) is considered. The sensi- tivity analysis results of the coal-fired power substitutions are shown in Fig. 5B. The environmental performances of direct land application are not affected by the electricity emission factor as there is no power substitution. As shown in Fig. 5B, both the gasification and AD ap- proaches can reach a negative life cycle GHG emission (-205.8 and -100.4 CO2-eq/kWh, respectively) when the avoided electricity is generated from the coal-fired power plant. The avoided electricity generation contributes to the gasification and AD approaches are -220.5, and -234.1 kg CO2-eq, respectively. If the energy mix in the future becomes cleaner given the technology advancements, the credit of avoided electricity will decrease. The life-cycle GHG emission of gasification and AD approaches will result in values higher than the base case in Fig. 5A.

Secondly, the uncertainties in energy efficiency are indicated by the CGE for gasification and biogas yield for AD (without further changes in the methane concentration in the biogas product). Based on the nu- merical simulation experiments, the CGE of gasification ranges from 60.9% to 79.2% when ER ranges from 0.1 to 0.6. The reference values of energy efficiencies are 78.4% (gasification CGE) and 0.3 m³ /kg volatile solid (biogas yield), which are employed in environmental performance analysis shown in Fig. 5A. In the sensitivity analysis, the gasification of CGE is ranged from 60.9% to 79.2% (by changing ER from 0.1 to 0.45) and the AD biogas yield is assumed to be range from 0.27 to 0.33 /kg volatile solid (+10% based on the reference biogas yield). As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the avoided electricity contribution increases with higher energy efficiency. However, the higher combustion emissions and lower carbon sequestrations offset the benefits of the larger power sub- stitutions and resulted in larger life cycle GHG emissions. The study by Jeswani et al. (2019) considered a poultry manure gasification plant with CHP in the UK and a GWP of 97.6 kg CO2-eq/tonne feedstock was reported (converted from 48.2 kg CO2-eq/MWh). The reason for the high value is due to the authors did not include the credit of carbon sequestration from biochar application.

32: <header>4.2. Environmental performance of the chicken manure gasification process </header>

The environmental performance is evaluated based on the indicators of ecosystem quality, climate change, and resource depletion. As shown in Fig. 4, gasification shows significant advantages in terms of climate change and resource depletion. It is important to note that better per- formance of resource depletion is portrayed through higher bars while the ecosystem quality and climate change indicators are through lower bars. The results of mid-point indicators and the characterization factors used for mid-point indicators are given in Figure A1 and Table A6 (Appendix), respectively.

The ecosystem quality results of the direct land application, AD, and gasification approaches are 204, 28.7, and -68.8 PDF m² year, respec- tively. As shown in Fig. 4, the direct land application approach has the worst performance when compared to the AD and gasification ap- proaches. This is mainly due to the nutrient flow. In the AD and gasifi- cation approaches, the avoided fertilizer provides high benefits. However, nutrient leaching and subsequent pollution have a significant negative impact on the performance of the direct land application. For the resource depletion indicator, the gasification approach performs favorably by a 260% improvement when compared to the direct land application (5948 and 1659 MJ, respectively). The amount of heat and power generated from gasification is more than its life cycle energy consumption.

The climate change results of the CM gasification approach, direct land application, and AD approach are -82.7, 76.2, and 38.3 kg CO2-eq/ tonne CM, respectively (as shown in Fig. 5A). In the land application scenario, although the nutrients (N, P, K) of CM can avoid utilization of synthetic fertilizer, the methane and N2O emission from raw CM has a higher impact on the indicator of the life cycle GHG emission. The GHG emission of avoided synthetic fertilizer shipping compromise a small portion of the net impact (-5.74 kg CO2-eq/tonne CM, which is inte- grated into the avoided fertilizer bar) but is 4.5 times higher than shipping raw CM to land application sites (1.28 kg CO2-eq/tonne CM, which is integrated into the manure emission bar). The mass of the avoided synthetic fertilizer is 5.8% of the CM mass but the transport distance is much longer (760 km, assumed import from Malaysia). In the gasification scenario, the negative GHG emission result of the

gasification approach is due to credits from the biochar carbon seques- tration, avoided fertilizer, and avoided electricity generation, which contributes -218.1, -85.1, and -97.4 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The positive part is the combustion emission in the exhaust gas of ICE. Similar to the gasification approach, the AD solution also has the credits of avoided fertilizer, avoided electricity, and carbon sequestration.

Among all the integrated GHG emission categories, combustion emission plays the most important role in both gasification and AD ap- proaches (44.2% and 32.6%, respectively), and the second-largest impact is found to be the carbon sequestration categories of biochar and dewatered digestate (30.3% and 18.7%, respectively). Although the net energy of generated syngas is 8.4% higher than the biogas, the electricity generation of gasification is only 2.1% higher than the AD approach. This is because the biogas (majorly methane) has a higher heating value and a subsequent better ICE efficiency when compared to syngas. The gasification approach shows a 42.7% improvement in terms of carbon sequestration when compared to the AD approach. This is due to biochar having a higher ratio of soil sequestration than dewatered digestate. Besides, the N2O emission from digestate (161.9 kg CO2-eq/ tonne CM) also offsets the overall environmental performance of the AD approach. Based on the life cycle GHG emission results of the 3 sce- narios, the gasification approach can play an important role in GHG emission reduction.

There are several parameters that could influence the environmental performance results and the impacts of the uncertainties are discussed in this subsection. The first uncertainty considered is the potential types of substitute energy. The environmental performance results shown in Fig. 5A are assumed to replace Singapore's grid power with an average operating margin emission factor (0.4085 CO2-eq/kWh). To further understand the effects of electricity substitution, the electricity derived from coal-fired power (1.0024 CO2-eq/kWh) is considered. The sensi- tivity analysis results of the coal-fired power substitutions are shown in Fig. 5B. The environmental performances of direct land application are not affected by the electricity emission factor as there is no power substitution. As shown in Fig. 5B, both the gasification and AD ap- proaches can reach a negative life cycle GHG emission (-205.8 and -100.4 CO2-eq/kWh, respectively) when the avoided electricity is generated from the coal-fired power plant. The avoided electricity generation contributes to the gasification and AD approaches are -220.5, and -234.1 kg CO2-eq, respectively. If the energy mix in the future becomes cleaner given the technology advancements, the credit of avoided electricity will decrease. The life-cycle GHG emission of gasification and AD approaches will result in values higher than the base case in Fig. 5A.

Secondly, the uncertainties in energy efficiency are indicated by the CGE for gasification and biogas yield for AD (without further changes in the methane concentration in the biogas product). Based on the nu- merical simulation experiments, the CGE of gasification ranges from 60.9% to 79.2% when ER ranges from 0.1 to 0.6. The reference values of energy efficiencies are 78.4% (gasification CGE) and 0.3 m³ /kg volatile solid (biogas yield), which are employed in environmental performance analysis shown in Fig. 5A. In the sensitivity analysis, the gasification of CGE is ranged from 60.9% to 79.2% (by changing ER from 0.1 to 0.45) and the AD biogas yield is assumed to be range from 0.27 to 0.33 /kg volatile solid (+10% based on the reference biogas yield). As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the avoided electricity contribution increases with higher energy efficiency. However, the higher combustion emissions and lower carbon sequestrations offset the benefits of the larger power sub- stitutions and resulted in larger life cycle GHG emissions. The study by Jeswani et al. (2019) considered a poultry manure gasification plant with CHP in the UK and a GWP of 97.6 kg CO2-eq/tonne feedstock was reported (converted from 48.2 kg CO2-eq/MWh). The reason for the high value is due to the authors did not include the credit of carbon sequestration from biochar application.

36: <header>4.3. Microbial risk performance of chicken manure gasification </header>

The bacterial pathogen results were shown in Fig. 6. It was notable to point out that after gasification, no DNA could be extracted from CM biochar, which indicated that no bacterial pathogens were recognized. Therefore, high-temperature gasification turned out to be a safe way to treat CM with syngas and biochar production and simultaneously destroyed the DNA structure of the bacterial pathogens.

The relative abundance of the 10 potentially pathogenic bacteria of the fresh CM and dry CM were shown in Fig. 6. The relative abundance of the total pathogenic bacteria was decreased from 38.61% to 23.68% in CM after the drying process. And after gasification, the generated biochar had no pathogenic bacteria at all as shown in Fig. 6. In detail, Fusobacteriaceae (21.81%), Ruminococcaceae (3.32%), and

Enterococcaceae (3.10%) were the three most abundant families in fresh CM. They were decreased to Fusobacteriaceae (5.75%) and Enter- ococcaceae (2.08%), while the Ruminococcaceae (4.20%) was increased in dry CM. Noted that the Enterobacteriaceae (7.54%) was the highest in dry CM, followed by Fusobacteriaceae (5.75%) and Lachnospiraceae (4.25%). However, the pathogenic family of Enterobacteriaceae (1.52%) in fresh CM was lower than that in dry CM. Similarly, the relative abundance of genus level of pathogenic bacteria was shown in Fig. 6B. It showed that the Fusobacterium (21.81%), Enterococcus (2.24%), and Gallicola (2.00%) were the major genus in fresh CM. While the most abundant pathogenic bacteria in the genus level was Escherichia-Shigella (7.44%), followed by Fusobacterium (5.75%) and Anaerosporobacter (3.58%). When comes to the species level, the abundance of pathogenic bacteria corresponded with the family and genus level. The species of
Fig. 7. Infection probability at various distances to manure application sites.

bacterium_mortiferung (21.81%), Peptostreptococcaceae_bacterium (2.43%), and Enterococcus cecorum (2.00%) were the top three in fresh CM, while these three species were reduced to 5.75%, 0.41%, and 1.64% in dry CM (as shown in Fig. 6C). However, the Caecibacterium, spor- oformans were increased from 1.38% to 3.58% in CM after drying. It concluded that after the drying process, the Enterobacteriaceae such as Escherichia-Shigella was enriched in CM samples. As all the pathogens are eliminated after the high-temperature gasification, a microbial risk analysis of CM direct land application is addressed in this subsection to illustrate the potential benefit of CM gasification. The exposure scenario discussed in this subsection simu- lates the potential bioaerosol deposition of raw CM to the surface of leafy green vegetables grown near the CM application site. Based on the po- tential pathogen results at the genus level, the Shigella is selected as the reference pathogen for QMRA. Other reference pathogen candidates such as Salmonella spp., Campylobacter spp., and E.coli 0157:H7 are re- ported to be negligible in the pathogen test result. An inverse dispersion model (Jahne al., 2016) is employed as the bioaerosol exposure model for pathogen emission and transportation, in which the emission rates of pathogens are based on a representative bacteria (Enterococcus spp.) adjusted by manure concentration. The pathogen loading rates on leafy green vegetables are determined based on the downwind distance to the CM application site, the reference pathogen abundance and human infectivity, and the leafy green vegetable surface area. The human infectivity (i.e., one-time successful infection probability) of the refer- ence pathogen (Shigella) is formulated by a B-Poisson distribution (Haas et al., 2014). The statistical results of QMRA are based on the maximum concentration of pathogens reached on downwind crops, the estimation of one-time ingested pathogen does, and one-time successful infection probability distribution of the reference pathogen (see Table A4 in Ap- pendix for detailed formulations). The maximum concentration was calculated based on the inverse model of bioaerosol emission rate and downwind distances (as shown in Table A5 in Appendix). The down- wind infection risk results (on a one-time consumption basis) for leafy green vegetables grown at 3 different distances to the manure down- stream application site are shown in Fig. 7. Considering an acceptable risk level of 1:10,000 (Regli et al., 1991; Jahne et al., 2016) (i.e., consuming the potential contaminated leafy green vegetables once will result in a 0.01% probability of infection), the

median risk will fall below 100 m distance. However, if considering the peak risk level, the acceptable downwind distance is above 1000 m. The microbial risk analysis results prove the safety of gasification as a CM waste management solution and show the benefit of CM gasification in terms of public health risks when compared to direct land applications.

bacterium_mortiferung (21.81%), Peptostreptococcaceae_bacterium (2.43%), and Enterococcus cecorum (2.00%) were the top three in fresh CM, while these three species were reduced to 5.75%, 0.41%, and 1.64% in dry CM (as shown in Fig. 6C). The exposure scenario discussed in this subsection simu- lates the potential bioaerosol deposition of raw CM to the surface of leafy green vegetables grown near the CM application site. The human infectivity (i.e., one-time successful infection probability) of the refer- ence pathogen (Shigella) is formulated by a B-Poisson distribution (Haas et al., 2014). The statistical results of QMRA are based on the maximum concentration of pathogens reached on downwind crops, the estimation of one-time ingested pathogen does, and one-time successful infection probability distribution of the reference pathogen (see Table A4 in Ap- pendix for detailed formulations). The maximum concentration was calculated based on the inverse model of bioaerosol emission rate and downwind distances (as shown in Table A5 in Appendix). The down- wind infection risk results (on a one-time consumption basis) for leafy green vegetables grown at 3 different distances to the manure down- stream application site are shown in Fig. 7. Considering an acceptable risk level of 1:10,000 (Regli et al., 1991; Jahne et al., 2016) (i.e., consuming the potential contaminated leafy green vegetables once will result in a 0.01% probability of infection), the

median risk will fall below 100 m distance. However, if considering the peak risk level, the acceptable downwind distance is above 1000 m. The microbial risk analysis results prove the safety of gasification as a CM waste management solution and show the benefit of CM gasification in terms of public health risks when compared to direct land applications.

41: Gasification is a promising technology for chicken manure manage- ment for both hazard removal and energy generation purposes. In this paper, an industrial-level case study of chicken manure gasification in Singapore is demonstrated and the energy efficiency, life cycle GHG emission, and microbial risk of the CM management solution were evaluated. The optimal cold gas efficiency of the CM gasification is found to be 79.2% with an equivalent ratio of 0.45. The gasification- based treatment of 1 tonne chicken manure can reduce 145 and 107 kg CO2-eq when compared to the direct land application and anaerobic digestion approaches. The result of quantitative microbial risk assess- ment shows that all the potential pathogenic hazards in chicken manure can be removed through the gasification process and therefore the biochar product is safe for further agricultural applications.
